context: Proverbs chapter 3, verses 11 and 12, notice what God tells us

context: Proverbs chapter 3, verses 11 and 12, notice what God tells us
point_summary: Divine chastening is an expression of God's love, not his anger — because he loves us too much to leave us to our own devices.

context: it says this in Proverbs 24 in verse number 10, if thou faint in the day of adversity, thy strength is small.
point_summary: Proverbs 24:10's warning that fainting in adversity reveals small strength is applied spiritually: those who serve God in their own power, rather than relying on Christ, will inevitably grow weary.

context: Proverbs tells us, boast not thyself of tomorrow. Why? No man knoweth what a day may bring forth.
point_summary: Because no one is guaranteed tomorrow, Proverbs 27:1's warning against boasting of the future grounds the urgent call to settle one's salvation and communicate final wishes without delay.

context: We know not what's on the morrow, according to Proverbs 27.
point_summary: The uncertainty of tomorrow, drawn from Proverbs 27, urges immediate response to the gift of eternal life, since life is a vapor and today is the day of salvation.

context: Trust in the Lord with all thine heart and lean not
point_summary: Trusting in Pharaoh's inferior strength rather than God's demonstrated power stands as the implicit rebuke behind Proverbs 3:5's call to trust the Lord with all the heart and lean not on human strength.

context: In all thy ways acknowledge him, and he shall direct thy paths
point_summary: Proverbs 3:5–6 is invoked as the positive counterpoint to Israel's failure — trusting God rather than seeking strength from a power already proven inferior to Him.

context: whoso findeth a wife, findeth a good thing and obtain the favor of the Lord
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: death and life are in the power of the tongue
point_summary: Words carry the power of life and death (Prov. 18:21), establishing why the contrast between the friends' miserable comfort and Job's offered strengthening carries genuine moral and spiritual weight.

context: Solomon said in Proverbs 18 and verse 21, death and life are in the power of the tongue.
point_summary: Words carry the power of death and life, as Solomon's proverb confirms, and Job's experience of being broken in pieces by his friends' speech illustrates that reality concretely.

context: When the righteous are in authority, notice what the Bible says, the people rejoice. But when the wicked beareth rule, the people mourn.
point_summary: The quality of human governance—whether righteous or wicked—directly determines whether the people under that rule rejoice or mourn, establishing the stakes for why Christ's coming reign matters.

context: righteousness exalteth a nation, but sin is a reproach to any people
point_summary: Proverbs 14:34 is read as a universal principle—not limited to Israel—declaring that righteousness elevates any nation while sin brings reproach upon any people, obligating Christians to let Scripture shape their vote.

context: these six things doth the Lord hate, yea, seven are an abomination unto him
point_summary: Proverbs 6:17's phrase "hands that shed innocent blood" is applied directly to abortion as its precise biblical description.

context: a proud look, a lying tongue, and notice, and hands that shed innocent blood
point_summary: Proverbs 6:17's phrase "hands that shed innocent blood" is applied directly to abortion, identifying it as a biblical description of the practice rather than a political position.

context: Righteousness exalteth a nation, but sin is a reproach to any people.
point_summary: Righteousness exalting a nation and sin being its reproach is applied as a direct indictment of abortion as the societal consequence of fornication and moral looseness.

context: the rich ruleth over the poor and the borrower is servant to the lender

context: the borrower is servant to the lender
point_summary: Proverbs 22:7 is applied as a biblical warning that national debt of nearly $36 trillion places America in a position of servitude to its lenders, demanding fiscal responsibility at every level.

context: Notice this truth in Proverbs 21 and verse number 1, the King's heart is in the hand of the Lord. As the rivers of water, He turneth it, the King's heart, whithersoever He will.
point_summary: Proverbs 21:1 is cited to affirm that God sovereignly directs the hearts of rulers, grounding the call to trust divine control over electoral outcomes rather than human conspiracy theories.

context: every Word of God is pure
point_summary: Every word of God being pure (Prov. 30:5) grounds the claim that Scripture is the authoritative, preserved standard by which believers can expose false doctrine and discern truth from error.

context: The first part of Proverbs 16 and verse number 7 says, when a man's ways please the Lord, when a man's ways please the Lord.
point_summary: When a man's ways please the Lord is cited as the basis for praying that God would teach us how to live acceptably before Him.

reference_text: Proverbs chapter one in verse number 10
context: my son, if sinners entice thee, consent thou not
point_summary: Proverbs 1:10's command "consent thou not" reinforces the call to refuse sinful enticements outright, because no such participation can bring honor to God or preserve a believer's testimony.

context: Are we, listen, iron sharpeneth iron. Are we going, running with the right crowd...
point_summary: Iron sharpens iron — the mutual influence of companions is unavoidable, making it essential to run with those who will challenge and build up one another in godly living.

context: labor not to be rich. Cease from thine own wisdom.
point_summary: Proverbs 23:4's command to "labour not to be rich" is paired with 1 Timothy 6:9 to warn that wealth is fleeting and the heart must not be set upon it.

context: our hearts are to be kept with all diligence for out of it are the issues of life
point_summary: Guarding the heart with all diligence (Proverbs 4) is cited as the reason not to set your heart on increasing riches, linking directly to Psalm 62:10's warning.

context: We'll be looking here at Proverbs 18 and verse number 10 as a springboard again this evening.
point_summary: Proverbs 18:10 serves as a springboard for a series examining how God's Old Testament names reveal distinct aspects of His character to His people.

context: the name of the Lord is a strong tower, the righteous runneth into it and is safe
point_summary: The divine names God used in the Old Testament are understood as self-revelations of His character, with Proverbs 18:10 anchoring the series' premise that knowing those names offers safety and encouragement to believers today.

context: whom the Lord loveth, he chasteneth
point_summary: Divine chastening of those he loves, drawn from Proverbs 3:12 alongside Hebrews, frames the sudden and certain judgment warned against in Revelation 3:3 as corrective discipline, not abandonment.

context: Notice in Proverbs chapter 30, we'll finish, or chapter 28, we'll finish with this tonight.
point_summary: God's pursuit of fellowship with his children is grounded in his readiness to pardon and forgive, desiring restored closeness more than his people themselves desire it.

context: The Bible says here in Proverbs 28 and verse number 13, he that covereth his sins shall prosper. not prosper.
point_summary: Covering sin brings no prosperity, but God—ready to pardon and full of compassion—desires restored fellowship, as David's failed attempt at concealment and Nathan's confrontation plainly demonstrate.

reference_text: He that covereth his sins shall not prosper. But notice, whoso confesseth and forsaketh them shall have mercy.
context: We see here the Bible says, He that covereth his sins shall not prosper. But notice, whoso confesseth and forsaketh them shall have mercy.
point_summary: Covering sin leads only to ruin, while confessing and forsaking it opens the door to God's mercy—the only two options available to every sinner.

context: I know that I said that we'd end in Proverbs 28, but in Psalm 51, I won't read the entirety of this Psalm.
point_summary: Covering sin brings no prosperity, but confession and forsaking it yields mercy — and every person faces that binary choice with their own sin.

context: Whoso confesseth shall have mercy. We just read it in Proverbs 28.
point_summary: Covering sin brings no prosperity, but confession brings mercy — the two options Proverbs 28 sets before every sinner, illustrated by David's prayer in Psalm 51.

context: A verse of Scripture, really two verses of Scripture that many of us have memorized, Proverbs 3, verses five and six. Trust in the Lord, how? All thine heart.
point_summary: Saul's disobedience illustrates the warning of Proverbs 3:5—leaning on one's own understanding means evaluating situations by culture and popular opinion rather than simply doing what God said.

context: And this is what? Lean not on an own understanding. In all thy ways, acknowledge him and he shall direct thy paths, right?
point_summary: Leaning on one's own understanding — as Saul did by deferring to the people — is set against the call of Proverbs 3:5–6 to acknowledge God in all ways and let Him direct the path.

context: Proverbs 28, the Bible says this in verse 13. He that covereth his sins shall not prosper.
point_summary: Covering sin brings no prosperity — a plain, straightforward warning that the sermon presents as self-evident proof that hidden sin cannot succeed, illustrated through David's failed attempt to conceal his adultery and murder.

context: The Bible says he that covereth his sins shall not prosper... But whoso confesseth and forsaketh them, the sins, shall have mercy.
point_summary: Covering sin guarantees failure to prosper, while confessing and forsaking sin guarantees mercy — the word "shall" making both outcomes equally certain.

context: The Bible says the way of transgressors is hard.
point_summary: The way of transgressors is hard (Prov. 13:15), illustrated here by the years of guilt and divine pressure bearing down on Jacob's sons for covering their sin against Joseph.

context: you cover your sin, you shall not prosper
point_summary: Covering sin brings no prosperity, but confession opens the door to mercy and forgiveness — a truth the sons of Jacob learned through years of hard consequence.

context: He that confesseth and forsaketh shall have mercy
point_summary: Covering sin brings no prosperity, but confessing and forsaking it brings mercy — making prompt, humble confession to God the only reasonable response to guilt.

context: The horse is prepared for the day of battle, but safety is of the Lord.
point_summary: Safety belongs to God alone, not to human scheming — a truth the sermon draws from Proverbs 21:31 to show that Isaac's deception was unnecessary because God's protection never depended on it.

context: he that covereth his sins shall not prosper. But whosoever, or whoso, excuse me, confesseth and forsaketh them, his sins, shall have mercy.
point_summary: Covering sin brings no prosperity, but confession and forsaking it opens the door to God's mercy — a contrast the sermon grounds in the examples of Rachel, Achan, and David.

context: The Bible tells us here in Proverbs 13 in verse number 10 that pride leads to contention.
point_summary: Pride is identified as the root cause of contention—in the world, homes, and churches—on the direct authority of Proverbs 13:10's declaration that only by pride cometh contention.

context: only by pride cometh contention, but the well-advised is wisdom
point_summary: Pride is identified as the root cause of contention—in the world, homes, and churches—on the basis that "only by pride cometh contention" (Proverbs 13:10).

context: pride goeth before destruction and in haughty spirit before a fall
point_summary: Pride's danger is illustrated through Nebuchadnezzar's fall: as Proverbs 16:18 warns, pride goes before destruction, a pattern the sermon traces directly into Daniel 4.

context: Pride goeth before destruction.
point_summary: Pride's destructive consequence, illustrated by Nebuchadnezzar's immediate judgment the moment he boasted, confirms that God actively opposes the self-exalting and brings them low.

reference_text: whoso covereth this sin shall not prosper

point_summary: Covering sin rather than confessing it brings a forfeiture of prosperity and joy, making self-examination before the Lord's Supper an occasion for confession, not abstention.

context: The Bible says in Proverbs 28, I told you that we'd get to this verse of Scripture as we conclude this evening.

context: he that covereth his sins shall not prosper: but whoso confesseth and forsaketh them shall have mercy.
point_summary: Covering sin guarantees the absence of prosperity, while confessing and forsaking sin secures mercy — a divine either/or that cannot be nullified.

context: the truth of Proverbs 21, a verse of Scripture that we've often referred to
point_summary: God's sovereignty over rulers' hearts—illustrated by Proverbs 21:1's image of the king's heart turned by God like rivers of water—grounds the command to pray for those in authority rather than merely criticize them.

context: the king's heart is in the hand of the Lord. As the rivers of water, He, God, turneth it whithersoever He will.
point_summary: God's sovereignty over rulers' hearts—illustrated from Ezra and Nehemiah—grounds the call to pray for those in authority rather than merely complain about them.

context: Proverbs tells us this in Proverbs 24 and verse number 29, and really the response that many people like to have when somebody does them wrong, in Proverbs 24 and verse 29, the Bible says, say not, I will do so to him as he hath done to me. I will render to the man according to his work.
point_summary: Proverbs 24:29 is cited as the natural, fleshly impulse to retaliate in kind — returning wrong for wrong — which Jesus's teaching on forgiveness directly confronts.

context: I'm mindful of what the wise man said in Proverbs 20 and verse number 6, the latter part of the verse, he says, but a faithful man, who can find?

context: The Bible says that righteousness exalteth the nation, but sin is a reproach to any people.

context: Because righteousness exalteth the nation, but sin is a reproach to any people.

context: he that covereth his sin shall not prosper. But what a joy to know that he that confesseth, he receives mercy.

context: My son, despise not thou the chastening of the Lord, nor faint when thou art rebuked of Him.

context: For whom the Lord loveth, He chasteneth, and scourgeth every son whom He receiveth.
point_summary: Divine chastening is presented as proof of God's love, contrasting the world's coddling notion of love with the biblical truth that correction is itself a demonstration of genuine, perfect love.

context: God says in Proverbs 6, how we can sow discord is absolutely with our mouth, with our speech, and it says that God hates it.

context: Yea, these six things that the Lord hate, yea, seven is an abomination.
point_summary: Sowing discord through speech is listed among the things God hates in Proverbs 6, underscoring that ungodly talk is not a minor offense but an abomination before Him.

context: Yea, these six things that the Lord hate, yea, seven is an abomination.
point_summary: Sowing discord through speech is listed among the things God hates in Proverbs 6, underscoring that ungodly talk is not a minor offense but an abomination before him.

context: Yea, these six things that the Lord hate, yea, seven is an abomination.
point_summary: Sowing discord through speech is listed among the things God hates in Proverbs 6, underscoring that careless or divisive talk is a serious offense against divine holiness.

context: in Proverbs chapter number seven in particular, it talks about the attire of an harlot
point_summary: Proverbs 7's depiction of the harlot's attire illustrates that clothing communicates a message, grounding a broader call to modesty as a matter of holy conduct.

context: notice in Proverbs 31 and verse number one the Bible says this the words of King Lemuel the prophecy that his mother taught him

context: give not thy strength unto women nor thy ways to that which destroyeth kings
point_summary: A mother's warning against giving strength to women — language drawn directly from verse 3 — anchors the broader instruction she imparts to her son as belonging wholly to God.

context: it is not for kings oh Lemuel it is not for kings to drink wine nor for princes strong drink

context: lest they drink and forget the law and pervert the judgment of any of the afflicted
point_summary: Kings who drink risk forgetting the law and perverting justice for the afflicted, making sobriety a matter of righteous rule, not merely personal discipline.

context: give strong drink unto him that is ready to perish or die and wine unto those that be of a heavy heart
point_summary: Strong drink is prescribed for the dying and brokenhearted as merciful relief, contrasting with the warning against kings who drink and thereby pervert justice.

context: let him drink and forget his poverty and remember his misery no more
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: open thy mouth for the dumb and the cause of all such as are appointed to destruction
point_summary: Kings are called to speak up for the voiceless and defend the cause of the poor and needy, a duty Lemuel's mother laid before him as part of her royal instruction.

context: open thy mouth judge righteously and plead the cause of the poor and needy

context: who can find a virtuous woman for her price is far above rubies

context: as you begin to study through proverbs chapter 31 it would seem as if lemuel was receiving this instruction

context: notice in Proverbs 4 and verse number 1 the first way that will respond is that we will hear instruction
point_summary: Proverbs 4:1 is presented as a call to hear instruction, with the sermon arguing that understanding instruction's value produces a willing, attentive response in the younger generation toward the older.

context: hear ye children the instruction of a father and attend to know understanding

context: for I give you good doctrine forsaking not my law
point_summary: Proverbs 4:2's declaration that the father gives "good doctrine" grounds the sermon's call to value instruction, identifying God as the ultimate Father whose teaching is profitable and worth actively seeking.

context: notice over in Proverbs chapter number 19
point_summary: Hearing and receiving instruction are distinguished as separate acts, with true reception defined as applying what is imparted, not merely listening to it.

context: hear counsel and receive instruction that thou mayest be wise in thy latter end
point_summary: Proverbs 19:20 distinguishes hearing from receiving instruction, arguing that true reception means applying what is taught, not merely listening to it.

context: when we know in Proverbs 31 the truth that his mother taught him
point_summary: Proverbs 31's record of a mother actively teaching her son underscores that given instruction must also be received and applied, not merely heard.

context: whoso loveth instruction loveth knowledge but he that hateth reproof is brutish
point_summary: Loving instruction and loving knowledge are inseparably linked, forming a cycle in which valuing instruction produces a desire to hear and receive it.

context: first statement of Proverbs 12 and verse number 1 whoso loveth instruction loveth knowledge
point_summary: Loving instruction and loving knowledge are inseparably linked, forming a cycle in which valuing instruction leads to hearing, receiving, and increasingly loving it.

context: we think about this truth of Proverbs chapter 31, and we think about the way in which the proverb begins, and again, most of us this morning are familiar with much of the content, but we see in that very first verse that the Bible says that His mother taught Him.
point_summary: Proverbs 31:1's opening phrase "his mother taught him" frames the entire chapter as maternal instruction, prompting reflection on what lasting truths any God-given instructor has imparted to those in their care.

context: As we think about this truth in Proverbs 31, what great content is provided to us there in that portion of scripture
point_summary: Proverbs 31 is presented as rich scriptural content organized around two themes: the vision required of those who instruct and the value that recipients must place upon instruction received.

context: trust in the Lord with all thine heart and lean not into thine own understanding
point_summary: Proverbs 3:5 is invoked as the "right answer" that believers know but fail to live, exposed by overwhelming circumstances that strip away self-reliance and force deliberate trust in God.

context: The name of the Lord is a strong tower.

context: The righteous runneth into it and is safe. What a great word picture that the Lord uses here with regard to speaking about the name of the Lord.
point_summary: The name "LORD" (all caps, denoting Yahweh/Jehovah) in Proverbs 18:10 pictures God as a strong tower into which the righteous run for safety, introducing a sermon survey of ten Old Testament names of God.

context: We find that there are men, there are people upon this earth that have titles and have positions and we find that the Bible says in Proverbs that the heart of the king is where? It's in the hand of the Lord. And He turns it however He desires
point_summary: Even earthly kings who set themselves against God remain under His sovereign control, for their hearts are in His hand and He turns them as He wills.

context: Proverbs, chapter number 15, verse number 1 is our text this evening. I've titled the message, The Power of Our Words, The Power of Our Words, Proverbs 15 verse number 1. The Bible says this, a soft answer turneth away wrath, but grievous words stir up anger.
point_summary: Words carry a dual power—capable of building up through encouragement and compliment, or tearing down through criticism and attack—as Proverbs 15:1 frames the sermon's central theme.

context: The Bible says a soft answer. Notice the thought there, a soft answer, something that is tender or delicate, the answer being the response. And no doubt we can understand how our words can have that tender or delicate answer that we would provide a response to someone and we find that the Bible says that a soft answer turneth away wrath.
point_summary: A soft answer—tender and delicate in tone—turns away wrath, while grievous, hurtful words provoke anger, illustrating the power of words to either build up or bring low.

context: Consider with me the counsel that we see in Proverbs 4 and verse number 24
point_summary: Corrupt, "froward" speech is something Scripture commands believers to actively put away and distance themselves from, framing godly communication as a matter of deliberate, disciplined avoidance.

context: In Proverbs 4 and verse number 24 the Bible says this, put away from thee a froward mouth and perverse lips put far from thee.
point_summary: Proverbs 4:24 is cited as direct biblical counsel to actively put away corrupt and perverse speech, reinforcing the sermon's call to guard the words we use.

context: I consider the truth that God gives us in Proverbs 18 and verse number 21, where there the Bible says this, death and life are in the power of the tongue. They that love it shall eat the fruit thereof.
point_summary: Death and life are in the power of the tongue, establishing that words carry ultimate consequence—they can either destroy or give life.

context: The observation that is given to us in Proverbs 21 and verse 23, where the Bible says, whoso keepeth his mouth and his tongue keepeth his soul from troubles.
point_summary: Guarding one's mouth and tongue is presented as the practical means by which a person keeps his soul from the troubles that uncontrolled speech brings.

context: whoso keepeth his mouth and his tongue keepeth his soul from troubles
point_summary: Guarding one's mouth and tongue is presented as a direct safeguard for the soul, with the verse offered as a passage worth memorizing because unguarded words repeatedly bring personal trouble.

context: the truth from Proverbs 31, my mother taught me
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: where the Bible says, a soft answer turns away wrath, but grievous words stir up anger.
point_summary: Responding with a soft answer rather than grievous words determines whether a conversation produces peace or escalates into wrath and anger.

context: Let me share a few verses with you in the book of Proverbs chapter 11 and verse number 12.
point_summary: Lacking wisdom shows itself in contemptuous speech toward a neighbor, while true understanding knows when to hold one's peace rather than speak.

context: He that is void of wisdom despiseth his neighbor. But of man of understanding, notice what it says, holdeth his peace.
point_summary: Wisdom, not the compulsion to "give someone a piece of your mind," is what restrains speech; a person of understanding holds his peace rather than voicing what is unnecessary.

context: Notice in Proverbs 15, it says here that he that holdeth, a man of understanding holdeth his peace.
point_summary: Wise speech is not merely about what is said but when — a word spoken "in due season" brings joy, and discerning that timing belongs to the Holy Spirit's guidance.

context: Proverbs 15 and verse number 23, the Bible says, A man hath joy by the answer of his mouth in a word spoken, notice this phrase, in due season. How good is it?
point_summary: Timely speech—words spoken "in due season"—requires Spirit-led discernment, since the Holy Spirit knows the right moment even when the speaker does not.

context: Notice in Proverbs chapter 25 and verse number 11.
point_summary: A word "fitly spoken" is interpreted as meaning delivered at the right moment, directly reinforcing the "due season" principle from Proverbs 15:23 that Spirit-led timing governs when words should be spoken.

context: In Proverbs 25 and verse number 11, going right along with this in due season from Proverbs 15 and verse 23, here the Bible says this, a word, fitly spoken, is like apples of gold and pictures of silver.
point_summary: A word "fitly spoken" carries the literal sense of being delivered at the right moment, reinforcing that the Holy Spirit's guidance determines when necessary words should be spoken.

context: In Proverbs 25 and verse number 11, going right along with this in due season from Proverbs 15 and verse 23, here the Bible says this, a word, fitly spoken, is like apples of gold and pictures of silver.
point_summary: Speaking a word "in due season" requires Spirit-led discernment about timing and setting, since the Holy Spirit—not the speaker—knows the right moment to deliver even a necessary word.

context: faithful are the wounds of a friend. But the kisses of an enemy are deceitful.
point_summary: Genuine friendship, the sermon argues, sometimes requires painful correction — and Proverbs 27:6 frames such wounds as "faithful," distinguishing a true friend's hard words from an enemy's flattering but deceitful kisses.

context: iron sharpeneth iron
point_summary: True friendship sometimes requires delivering difficult but necessary words, and a genuinely biblical friend's honest correction—however painful—is faithful rather than deceitful.

context: faithful are the wounds of a friend
point_summary: Genuine friendship sometimes requires delivering difficult but necessary words, and the faithfulness of such "wounds" distinguishes a true friend from an enemy whose flattering "kisses" are deceitful.

context: a soft answer turneth away wrath, but grievous words stir up anger
point_summary: A soft answer that turns away wrath requires God's help to deliver, and the listener's response to difficult but necessary words is equally vital.

context: And if we're not careful, we don't take heed to the truth of Proverbs 21 and verse 23. And our words, they cause us trouble, cause us trouble.
point_summary: Guarding one's mouth and tongue, as Proverbs 21:23 teaches, is presented as the practical safeguard that keeps a person's words from causing trouble in volatile, real-life confrontations.

context: the just man falleth seven times, but what does he do? He rises up again.
point_summary: Even when the righteous fall repeatedly, Proverbs 24:16 assures they rise again—applied here as a call to refuse a permanent state of defeat and to be overcomers through God's promised victory.

context: Proverbs 27 in verse number one, the wise man gives us insight.
point_summary: Proverbs 27:1's reminder that days are numbered grounds the call to treat each day's minutes like a finite, unspendable sum—time is a precious commodity that must be redeemed, not squandered.

context: Boast not thyself of tomorrow for thou knowest not what a day may bring forth.
point_summary: Because no one knows what tomorrow holds, Proverbs 27:1 is cited to underscore that time is a precious, uncertain commodity demanding wise, present investment rather than presumptuous delay.

context: He that is of a proud heart stirreth up strife. But notice what it says. He that putteth his trust in the Lord shall be made fat.
point_summary: Proverbs 28:25–26 is read as a direct biblical refutation of the cultural counsel to "follow your heart," contrasting the fool who trusts his own heart with the one who trusts the Lord.

context: He that trusteth in his own heart is a fool. Is that what your Bible says? I'm not making it up, amen? That's what the Word of God says. But whoso walketh wisely, he shall be delivered.
point_summary: Trusting your own heart is foolishness, not wisdom — Proverbs 28:26 contrasts the fool who follows his heart with the one who trusts the Lord and walks wisely.

context: turn back to the fourth chapter if you will, chapter number four
point_summary: Proverbs 4:23 is cited as the believer's active response to the warning against deception: guarding the heart with diligent, faithful loyalty, because from it flow the issues of life.

context: Notice in verse number 23 of Proverbs 4, it's a very simple verse of Scripture, but the Bible says this, keep thy heart with all diligence for out of it are the issues of life.
point_summary: Because the heart is deceitful and the source of life's issues, Proverbs 4:23 calls believers to guard it with diligence—actively watching and protecting it with fidelity through the Word of God.
